script.instruction: Find a comfortable seat. Spine erect but relaxed. Close your eyes gently.
Take a deep breath in... and let it out slowly. One more time—in... and out.
Now let the breath find its natural rhythm. Don't control it. Just notice it.
Feel the breath entering your nostrils. Cool air coming in. Warm air going out.
When your mind wanders—and it will—gently bring attention back to the breath. No judgment. Just return.
Keep watching. Breathing is happening. You are watching it happen.
Notice the slight pause between inhale and exhale. And between exhale and inhale. Those pauses are doorways to stillness.
Let everything else fall away. Right now, there is only this breath. This moment.
Gently deepen your breath. Begin to feel your body again. The sounds around you.
When you're ready, open your eyes. Stay in this space for a moment before returning to activity.

isha_kriya_basic.title: I Am Not The Body, I Am Not The Mind
isha_kriya_basic.purpose: Creating space between you and your identifications

script.instruction: Sit with spine comfortably erect. Close your eyes.
Take three deep breaths to settle. In... out... In... out... In... out.
Now, with each inhale, mentally say: 'I am not the body.'
With each exhale, mentally say: 'I am not even the mind.'
Don't try to believe these words. Just use them as pointers. Inhale—'I am not the body.' Exhale—'I am not even the mind.'
Continue at your own pace. Let the words create space between you and what you usually identify with.
Keep going. If the mind wanders, return to the words. Inhale—'I am not the body.' Exhale—'I am not even the mind.'
Now let go of the words. Just sit. Whatever happens, let it happen. Whatever you experience, just be with it.
Slowly bring awareness back to your body. Feel your breath. Feel where you're sitting.
Open your eyes gently. Stay with this quality of attention as you move into your day.
Lie down or sit comfortably. Let your body be supported by the surface beneath you.
Close your eyes. Take a few deep breaths and let your body settle.
Bring attention to the top of your head. Notice any sensations there—tingling, pressure, warmth. Don't judge. Just notice.
Move attention to your face. Forehead... eyes... cheeks... jaw. Where there's tension, let it soften.
Feel your neck and throat. Let any tightness release with your exhale.
Shoulders... let them drop away from your ears. Let them be heavy.
Move down your arms... upper arms... elbows... forearms... wrists... hands... each finger. Let them be completely relaxed.
Bring attention to your chest. Feel your heart beating. Feel your breath moving your ribcage. Let the chest be soft.
Move to your belly. Let it be completely relaxed—no holding. The belly can be soft.
Feel your lower back... hips... pelvis. Let this whole area release.
Move down your legs... thighs... knees... calves... ankles... feet... each toe. Let everything be heavy and relaxed.
Now feel your whole body at once. A field of sensations, all relaxed, all held by the earth.
Stay here. There's nothing to do. Nowhere to go. Just rest.
When you're ready, begin to deepen your breath. Move your fingers and toes gently. And when you're ready, open your eyes.
Sit comfortably. Close your eyes. Take a few settling breaths.
Bring to mind the difficult emotion you've been experiencing. You don't need to figure it out—just let it be present.
Where do you feel this emotion in your body? Chest? Throat? Stomach? Find where it lives.
Breathe into that area. Not to fix it or make it go away—just to be with it. As if saying: 'I see you. You're allowed to be here.'
Notice the quality of the sensation. Is it heavy? Sharp? Hot? Tight? Just observe without needing to change it.
If stories start—about why this happened, what it means—gently set them aside. Come back to the raw sensation.
The emotion wants to be felt, not fixed. Can you let it be felt? Can you give it space?
Notice: you are feeling the emotion, but you are not the emotion. You are the awareness that contains it. The emotion is temporary. You are here.
Let the intensity be whatever it is. It might increase, it might decrease. Both are fine. Just stay present.
Now take a deep breath. Acknowledge yourself for being willing to feel what's difficult. That takes courage.
Let the focus on the emotion soften. Return to awareness of your whole body. Your breath. This room.
Open your eyes when ready. The emotion may still be there—that's okay. You've practiced being with it. That's the work.
Lie down comfortably. Let your body settle into rest.
Close your eyes. Take three slow breaths, letting go of the day with each exhale.
Bring to mind one specific thing from today that you're grateful for. Something small is fine. A meal. A moment. A person.
Let yourself really feel the gratitude. Not just think it—feel it in your body. Where do you feel appreciation?
Now bring to mind a second thing. Again, specific. Let gratitude fill you.
And a third. Three points of gratitude from this one day. Feel them all together.
Let this feeling of appreciation spread through your whole body. Gratitude is not scarce—it expands when you give attention to it.
As you drift toward sleep, let gratitude be the last thing you feel. It will color your rest.
Sleep well. You've practiced the most important thing: appreciation for being alive.

delivery_notes.pace: Slower than normal conversation. Let pauses breathe.
delivery_notes.tone: Calm but not drowsy. Grounded. Present.
delivery_notes.voice: Softer volume. Steady rhythm. No rushing.
